crux: Don't construct complex objects directly -- use a Builder as a staging area, configure step-by-step, and produce the final validated object only when you say build().
oneLiner: Separate the construction of a complex object from its representation so the same construction process can create different representations -- configure freely in a Builder, validate cross-field constraints, compute derived values, and produce a validated Product only at build() time.
problemStatement: Imagine a restaurant ordering system. A customer orders a customizable meal: bun type, patty count, cheese type, veggies (lettuce, tomato, onion, jalapeno -- any combo), sauces (ketchup, mayo, BBQ -- any combo), extras (bacon, egg, avocado), and meal size. That's 15+ parameters. Telescoping constructors are unreadable. Setters leave the object in transient invalid states -- half-built meals floating around. Someone mixes up parameter order and a customer gets a disgusting burger. Tomorrow the restaurant adds 'spice level' -- every existing constructor call breaks.

analogy.title: The Subway Sandwich Counter
analogy.description: You walk into Subway and describe your sandwich step by step: bread type, meat, cheese, veggies, sauces. You can change your mind mid-way -- 'actually, swap swiss for cheddar.' The sandwich artist (Builder) collects your choices on an open sub. Only when you say 'That's it, wrap it up!' does the sandwich become a sealed, finished product. You never touch the raw ingredients yourself.

mapping.'Wrap it up!': build() method -- validates and produces final product

participants.name: Product
participants.role: The complex object being constructed. Private constructor -- only Builder can create it. Identity fields are typically final; lifecycle fields can be mutable with controlled access.
participants.example: Meal (with bun, pattyCount, cheese, toppings, sauce, size, price)

participants.name: Builder
participants.role: The staging area that collects configuration step by step via fluent setters. Returns 'this' from each setter for chaining. The build() method validates cross-field constraints, computes derived values, and creates the validated Product.

participants.name: Director (optional)
participants.role: Encapsulates reusable 'recipes' -- predefined build sequences. Calls builder methods in a specific order. Multiple directors can use the same builder to create different product variants.
participants.example: MealRecipes.kidsMeal(builder) -- sets small bun, 1 patty, juice, toy

participants.name: Client
participants.role: Creates a Builder, configures it with fluent calls, and calls build(). Never touches the Product constructor directly.
